# JOS-S63 — Agent Conduct Rulebook Standard

## Purpose

Define what a well-formed **agent-conduct rulebook** looks like: the governed
body of rules that says *how* an agent of a given type conducts itself,
independent of its job. FAS calls the subsystem that holds and runs this
rulebook **Behavior** (subsystem code `behavior`); this standard is the JOS
definition of the rulebook's shape and doctrine. It exists so conduct is
authored once and attached to many by type and capability — never hardwired in
code, never trapped in one agent's SOPs, never hand-copied at birth.

This standard describes quality (what GOOD looks like) and doctrine. The
binding runtime schema — table columns, resolver internals, the enforcement
substrate — is FAS's to specify and run (Scope B); this standard cites it, it
does not duplicate it.

> **Doctrine standard.** This is a doctrine/quality standard under JOS-S37: S37 §4 and §5 collapse into the single "Quality criteria" section below rather than being authored as separate sections. Date reconciliation: D-216 was ratified as a decision **2026-07-15**; the Tier-0 **rule content** was ratified **2026-07-16** (this standard's creation date). References to "ratified 2026-07-15" mean the decision; the rules themselves land under the 2026-07-16 content ratification.

## Boundary test (before authoring against this standard)

A rule belongs in the conduct rulebook ONLY IF all hold:

- It is **conduct**, not work. It says *how to behave* ("never state an error
  as if it were content"; "in chat, thread replies and match business-hours
  manner"), not *what job to do*. A duty, area, or SOP is WORK — it lives in the
  duty layer, not here.
- It is **type- or capability-wide**, not agent-specific. If it is true only of
  one named agent's job, it is an SOP, not a conduct rule.
- It is **not a WORK-axis exception**. A persistent "when discharging a duty,
  you may or must act thus" bound on a specific gate — a D-215 **standing order**
  or **autonomy grant** — is a WORK-axis mechanism (`operations:standing-order`)
  and stays there. Conduct is the type/capability layer that applies regardless
  of which duty is running.

If it is a binary "exists / does not exist" scanner check → author a Rule. If it
is a downstream data contract → that is FAS's enforcement schema, not this
standard. If it commands ("always do X") → that is a Standing Order; state
conduct as "conduct is well-formed when …".

## Quality criteria — what a well-formed conduct rulebook looks like

A conduct rulebook is well-formed when it exhibits all four properties below.

### 1. Three tiers — "everyone / your kind / your tools"

The rulebook resolves as an **ordered composition of tiers**, minimally three:

- **Constitutional (Tier 0)** — applies to *every* agent, no exceptions, and
  cannot be overridden by anything below it. These are the safety kernel:
  never fabricate a tool result; an error is never spoken to a human as if it
  were content; fail closed when the governing rules cannot be loaded; never
  send ungated to a non-principal. Because promotion of these invariants from
  code into governed rows makes the previously-immutable editable, a well-formed
  rulebook gives Tier 0 a materially **higher change ceremony** than lower tiers
  (dual-control change, a cooling period, and *more* staged rollout, not less —
  a wrong constitutional rule is the highest-blast-radius artifact on the
  platform) and keeps a small **code kernel**: a hand-verified, code-enforced
  duplicate of the few most catastrophic invariants that fires even if the whole
  rulebook pipeline is compromised. Rules-as-data is a visibility principle, not
  a licence to delete the last deterministic backstop.
- **Type pack (Tier 1)** — applies by **agent type**, with inheritance (a
  partner type inherits a specialist's base and adds to it), so a rule true of a
  whole kind of agent is written once.
- **Capability pack (Tier 2)** — attaches **if and only if** the agent holds the
  corresponding tool or channel. Chat-manner conduct attaches only to agents
  that can post to chat; a capability's guard rules arrive and depart *with* the
  capability.

Three tiers is the minimum that expresses "everyone / your kind / your tools."
It is well-formed to treat "three" as the *authoring* model while composing an
**ordered list of packs** at resolution — so a future overlay (tenant,
jurisdiction, incident hotfix) is an addition, not a rewrite. Resolution is
**dynamic, not birth-only**: a capability granted or revoked mid-life
re-resolves the applicable packs, so a tool never exists without its guard
rules.

### 2. Linked, not copied

Agents hold **references** to their packs, resolved from the central rulebook —
never a frozen copy of rule text taken at birth. Edit the type pack once and
every agent of that type updates; there is no copy-and-migrate step (the thing
that makes human handbooks rot). Linking is a data operation: a rule change
propagates to running agents without rebuilding or redeploying the runtime.

Because instant, invisible global propagation is a footgun on a system with real
enforcement, a well-formed rulebook pairs linking with **immutable, hashed
per-session bundles ("policy epochs")**. The rule you *deploy* propagates by
link; the rule that *governed a specific past action* is pinned to an epoch and
reconstructable for audit and replay. A capability change (or an emergency
rollback) closes one epoch and opens the next as a logged, attributable event,
and each action record pins the epoch it was decided under. Edits reach
production through release management — draft → conflict analysis → replay →
canary → staged rollout → rollback path — not by overwriting every agent at once.

### 3. Asymmetric precedence — safety wins downward, every denial attributable

Precedence is **asymmetric**:

- **Permissions** (*may* the agent do X) compose by **AND across tiers**: every
  applicable tier must agree before the act is allowed.
- **Prohibitions** (*must never* do Y) compose by **OR across tiers**: any tier
  may forbid, and nothing below can un-forbid.

The overall priority ladder is **Constitutional safety > governance / compliance
> WORK duty or SOP > task helpfulness**. A lower tier may *add* a restriction but
may never *pierce* a constitutional one — this is what "safety wins downward"
means. And every deny or hold is **attributable**: it carries the specific rule
id and tier that caused it, surfaced to observability. (What the agent itself is
told may be reduced to the rule's category and remedy rather than its exact
predicate, so a boundary is not handed to an adversary to search around — but the
full attribution is always recorded.) A default deny — where no rule matched — is
distinguishable from a forbid-caused deny by its empty determining set.

### 4. JOS defines, FAS runs

Ownership is split and cross-referenced, never duplicated in substance:

- **JOS owns the rulebook** — the content and the schema of the tiers
  (constitutional rules, type packs, capability packs) live as governed JOS
  artifacts. Authoring, versioning, review cadence, and change ceremony are JOS's.
  The rulebook tables are platform-global / JOS-owned (no tenant scoping on the
  definitions themselves).
- **FAS runs the rulebook** — a FAS-side mirror of it, the birth-time and
  capability-change **resolver**, runtime injection of the resolved conduct, and
  the **gate** that enforces the safety-critical rules at every side-effect
  boundary. Nothing is hand-authored per agent in FAS; runtime records (which
  rule fired, under which epoch) are tenant-scoped.

This is the platform's "JOS defines, FAS runs" principle applied to conduct. The
same decision is therefore doctrine in both repos: this standard in JOS, and the
Behavior subsystem doctrine in FAS.

## What a conduct rule must express (rule shape — quality level)

This standard is quality, not schema; the binding schema is FAS's (Scope B). At
the doctrine level, a well-formed conduct rule is **data, not embedded prose**,
and expresses at least: a stable **id**; the **tier** it belongs to; a governed
**category**; its **scope** (the type or capability that attaches it); a
**version**; and an **enforcement reference** binding the human-readable rule to
the artifact that enforces it. The human-readable rule and the enforced artifact
must be **traceably** the same — the same governed source, versioned together and
testable against each other — a deliberately weaker and more honest claim than
"provably the same." A rule compiles at resolution into a directive block
injected into context (soft enforcement) and/or a machine check at the gate
(hard enforcement).

## Anti-patterns

- **Conduct in code.** A universal rule ("never fabricate") written only as an
  inline code check is invisible to governance. Promote it to a governed Tier-0
  row — while keeping the code kernel as the deterministic backstop.
- **Conduct in one agent's SOPs.** Chat manner trapped in a single agent's
  duty is not reusable. It is a capability pack, keyed to the chat capability.
- **Copy-at-birth.** Snapshotting rule text into each agent at creation turns
  every edit into a migration across every agent. Link; pin epochs for audit.
- **Symmetric precedence.** Treating permissions and prohibitions the same (or
  letting a lower tier un-forbid a constitutional prohibition) breaks safety.
  Permissions AND upward; prohibitions OR upward; safety wins downward.
- **Silent denial.** A deny with no attributable rule id and tier is
  unauditable and unexplainable. Every denial names its cause.
- **Hard-coding "three tiers" as a constant.** The tier *count* is the authoring
  model; the *composition* is an ordered list of packs so overlays can be added
  without a rewrite.
- **Filing conduct as a WORK-axis standing order.** Generic, type-wide conduct
  does not belong in a per-duty D-215 standing order; that is the conflation this
  rulebook removes. Standing orders remain WORK-axis exceptions.

## Worked examples

**Positive — a capability pack.** Chat conduct ("thread replies; match
business-hours manner; never state an error as content") is authored once as a
capability pack keyed to the chat-write capability. Every agent that holds
chat-write resolves it automatically; an agent that never gains chat-write never
carries it; revoking chat-write removes it in the same transaction. The rule is
edited in one place and every chat-capable agent updates by link.

**Anti-pattern → its repair.** The flagship Chief-of-Staff agent's chat manners
were originally written inside her own SOPs — trapped in her job, invisible to
the next agent, and hand-copied if reused. Under this standard the conduct is
lifted out of her SOPs into the chat capability pack (conduct) and the residual
duty-specific exceptions become WORK-axis standing orders (D-215). Nothing is
deleted until its governed replacement is live and verified; nothing hardwired
survives the migration.

## Validation

- **FAS enforcement** is the operative check: the resolver attaches the right
  packs by type and capability, the gate enforces the constitutional and
  prohibition rules at every side-effect boundary, and every denial is logged
  with its rule id, tier, and policy epoch.
- **Assurance plane (FAS, Scope B).** A conflict / unsatisfiability linter over
  the composed rule set (does precedence + inheritance + AND/OR yield a set no
  agent can satisfy?), rule-fire observability, policy versioning with canary +
  rollback, and a shadow-replay harness are the machine that keeps the rulebook
  honest; a well-formed rulebook is verified by them before deploy.
- **JOS gate (forthcoming).** A frontmatter-and-doctrine conformance gate for
  this standard follows the JOS Dog-Food gate pattern; until it ships, author
  discipline plus this standard's reading.
